item.title: C-DiffSET: Leveraging Latent Diffusion for SAR-to-EO Image Translation with Confidence-Guided Reliable Object Generation
item.dc:creator: Jeonghyeok Do
Jaehyup Lee
Seungchul Lee
Munchurl Kim
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery provides robust environmental and temporal coverage (e.g., during clouds, seasons, day-night cycles), yet its noise and unique structural patterns pose interpretation challenges, especially for non-experts. SAR-to-EO (Electro-Optical) image translation (SET) has emerged to make SAR images more perceptually interpretable. However, traditional approaches, trained from scratch on limited SAR-EO datasets, are prone to overfitting and struggle with dataset inconsistencies. To address these challenges, we introduce Confidence Diffusion for SAR-to-EO Translation (C-DiffSET), a framework leveraging a pretrained Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) to effectively adapt its extensive generative priors from natural images to the EO domain. Our investigation reveals that the pretrained VAE encoder effectively aligns SAR and EO images within a shared latent space, demonstrating robustness even to varying noise levels in SAR inputs. To further improve pixel-wise fidelity for SET and mitigate artifacts from temporal discrepancies, such as appearing or disappearing objects, we propose a novel confidence-guided diffusion (C-Diff) loss. This loss dynamically guides the diffusion process to down-weight penalties in uncertain regions, thereby enhancing structural accuracy. C-DiffSET achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on multiple benchmark datasets (QXS-SAROPT, SAR2Opt, SpaceNet6, Stellar-Vision), significantly outperforming recent image-to-image translation methods and specialized SET methods across all standard metrics. The source code and trained models are available at https://github.com/KAIST-VICLab/C-DiffSET. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.832 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jeonghyeok Do; Jaehyup Lee; Seungchul Lee; Munchurl Kim

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3701447

Relevance: 0.832 (must_read)

Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery provides robust environmental and temporal coverage (e.g., during clouds, seasons, day-night cycles), yet its noise and unique structural patterns pose interpretation challenges, especially for non-experts. SAR-to-EO (Electro-Optical) image translation (SET) has emerged to make SAR images more perceptually interpretable. However, traditional approaches, trained from scratch on limited SAR-EO datasets, are prone to overfitting and struggle with dataset inconsistencies. To address these challenges, we introduce Confidence Diffusion for SAR-to-EO Translation (C-DiffSET), a framework leveraging a pretrained Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) to effectively adapt its extensive generative priors from natural images to the EO domain. Our investigation reveals that the pretrained VAE encoder effectively aligns SAR and EO images within a shared latent space, demonstrating robustness even to varying noise levels in SAR inputs. To further improve pixel-wise fidelity for SET and mitigate artifacts from temporal discrepancies, such as appearing or disappearing objects, we propose a novel confidence-guided diffusion (C-Diff) loss. This loss dynamically guides the diffusion process to down-weight penalties in uncertain regions, thereby enhancing structural accuracy. C-DiffSET achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on multiple benchmark datasets (QXS-SAROPT, SAR2Opt, SpaceNet6, Stellar-Vision), significantly outperforming recent image-to-image translation methods and specialized SET methods across all standard metrics. The source code and trained models are available at https://github.com/KAIST-VICLab/C-DiffSET.

item.title: Single-Stage Instance Segmentation Survey: A “1+2+3” Technical Framework
item.dc:creator: Tao Zhou
Defang Chang
Huiling Lu
Wenwen Chai
Ke Song
Feiyu Shen
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Single-stage instance segmentation is a prominent research direction in computer vision. However, in existing single-stage instance segmentation methods, there is a lack of summary on the 6 key questions: “How to select the learning paradigm?”, “How to extract features?”, “How to fuse features?”, “How to classify objects?”, “How to generate object masks?”, and “How to localize objects?”. These questions restrict the development and application to some degree. To address these questions, this paper proposes a “1+2+3” technical framework for summarizing single-stage instance segmentation. “1” represents one learning paradigm. Four mainstream learning paradigms and selection strategies are systematically summarized. “2” represents two local structures. The three characteristics of feature extraction are summarized, and the two feature fusion strategies are summarized. “3” represents three aspects of instance prediction. The object classification, object mask generation methods and object localization are discussed. Furthermore, the paper discusses new technologies such as instance segmentation based on promptable foundation models and instance segmentation based on vision-language models, as well as their typical applications in medical, video, and remote sensing image segmentation. The “1+2+3” technology framework proposed in the paper provides a problem-oriented technical map for instance segmentation, promoting the further development of single-stage instance segmentation. Published: 2026-06-09T19:52:16+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.823 (must_read)

Authors: Tao Zhou; Defang Chang; Huiling Lu; Wenwen Chai; Ke Song; Feiyu Shen

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3701328

Relevance: 0.823 (must_read)

Single-stage instance segmentation is a prominent research direction in computer vision. However, in existing single-stage instance segmentation methods, there is a lack of summary on the 6 key questions: “How to select the learning paradigm?”, “How to extract features?”, “How to fuse features?”, “How to classify objects?”, “How to generate object masks?”, and “How to localize objects?”. These questions restrict the development and application to some degree. To address these questions, this paper proposes a “1+2+3” technical framework for summarizing single-stage instance segmentation. “1” represents one learning paradigm. Four mainstream learning paradigms and selection strategies are systematically summarized. “2” represents two local structures. The three characteristics of feature extraction are summarized, and the two feature fusion strategies are summarized. “3” represents three aspects of instance prediction. The object classification, object mask generation methods and object localization are discussed. Furthermore, the paper discusses new technologies such as instance segmentation based on promptable foundation models and instance segmentation based on vision-language models, as well as their typical applications in medical, video, and remote sensing image segmentation. The “1+2+3” technology framework proposed in the paper provides a problem-oriented technical map for instance segmentation, promoting the further development of single-stage instance segmentation.

item.title: HPHF-MMDet: Heterogeneous Perception and Hyperspherical Fusion for Multimodal Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Guohua Lv
Zhengyang Zhang
Guangxiao Ma
Yongbiao Gao
Jihao Jin
Qian Xu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Multimodal object detection aims to leverage the complementary characteristics of visible (RGB) and infrared (IR) images to address perception challenges in complex environments. However, existing methods typically utilize structurally uniform backbones that neglect the intrinsic disparities in imaging mechanisms between RGB and IR modalities. Moreover, existing fusion methods fail to effectively address the feature distribution discrepancies across heterogeneous modalities. To address these issues, we propose Heterogeneous Perception and Hyperspherical Fusion for Multimodal Object Detection (HPHF-MMDet). We devise a Modality-Specific Heterogeneous Backbone (MSHB) that diverges from the conventional symmetric feature extraction paradigm. Specifically, for the RGB modality, we employ a Context-Guided Detail Enhancement Module (CGDEM) to dynamically modulate detailed features, suppressing background interference while highlighting target textures. For the IR modality, a Multi-Scale Windmill Convolution (MSWC) is carefully designed to exploit asymmetric receptive fields for capturing the morphological features of thermal targets. Furthermore, to bridge the feature distribution discrepancies between heterogeneous modalities, we introduce a Cross-Modal Complementary Hyperspherical Fusion (CM-CHF) mechanism, which aggregates complementary information through bidirectional hyperspherical alignment. We conduct extensive experiments on the LLVIP, M3FD, and MFAD datasets. Our method achieves mAP50 of 97.9%, 89.8%, and 80.8% respectively, significantly outperforming existing state-of-the-art approaches. The source code is available at https://github.com/zzyadad/HPHF-MMDet. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.813 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guohua Lv; Zhengyang Zhang; Guangxiao Ma; Yongbiao Gao; Jihao Jin; Qian Xu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3701598

Relevance: 0.813 (must_read)

Multimodal object detection aims to leverage the complementary characteristics of visible (RGB) and infrared (IR) images to address perception challenges in complex environments. However, existing methods typically utilize structurally uniform backbones that neglect the intrinsic disparities in imaging mechanisms between RGB and IR modalities. Moreover, existing fusion methods fail to effectively address the feature distribution discrepancies across heterogeneous modalities. To address these issues, we propose Heterogeneous Perception and Hyperspherical Fusion for Multimodal Object Detection (HPHF-MMDet). We devise a Modality-Specific Heterogeneous Backbone (MSHB) that diverges from the conventional symmetric feature extraction paradigm. Specifically, for the RGB modality, we employ a Context-Guided Detail Enhancement Module (CGDEM) to dynamically modulate detailed features, suppressing background interference while highlighting target textures. For the IR modality, a Multi-Scale Windmill Convolution (MSWC) is carefully designed to exploit asymmetric receptive fields for capturing the morphological features of thermal targets. Furthermore, to bridge the feature distribution discrepancies between heterogeneous modalities, we introduce a Cross-Modal Complementary Hyperspherical Fusion (CM-CHF) mechanism, which aggregates complementary information through bidirectional hyperspherical alignment. We conduct extensive experiments on the LLVIP, M3FD, and MFAD datasets. Our method achieves mAP50 of 97.9%, 89.8%, and 80.8% respectively, significantly outperforming existing state-of-the-art approaches. The source code is available at https://github.com/zzyadad/HPHF-MMDet.

item.title: BPA-Net: Bidirectional Prototype Alignment for Image Captioning
item.dc:creator: Tianci Li
Cheng Xu
Yizhe Song
Jiazheng Yuan
Songyin Dai
Jiancheng Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Image captioning aims to generate accurate textual descriptions for visual inputs. A major challenge is the semantic gap between continuous visual features and discrete linguistic concepts. Existing methods often rely on the unidirectional injection of external textual priors, which inevitably introduces semantic noise. Furthermore, CLIP-based image captioning methods lack direct supervision from ground-truth captions, leading to weak cross-modal alignment. To address these issues, we propose BPA-Net, a framework for bidirectional prototype alignment. Specifically, the Dual Prototype Extraction module independently projects visual features and ground-truth text tokens into a shared semantic space to form structured prototypes. Then, the Cross-Modal Prototype Alignment module enables bidirectional interaction and alignment between visual and textual prototypes guided by symmetric matching objectives. This approach effectively mitigates the noise inherent in external proxy texts and establishes robust semantic alignment. Extensive experiments on the MS-COCO, Flickr8k, and Flickr30k benchmarks demonstrate that BPA-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance. Furthermore, evaluations on the Nocaps dataset confirm its strong generalization capability for describing novel objects. The code is available at https://github.com/CarasuLee/BPA-Net. Published: 2026-06-10T20:04:43+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.813 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tianci Li; Cheng Xu; Yizhe Song; Jiazheng Yuan; Songyin Dai; Jiancheng Zhang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3701826

Relevance: 0.813 (must_read)

Image captioning aims to generate accurate textual descriptions for visual inputs. A major challenge is the semantic gap between continuous visual features and discrete linguistic concepts. Existing methods often rely on the unidirectional injection of external textual priors, which inevitably introduces semantic noise. Furthermore, CLIP-based image captioning methods lack direct supervision from ground-truth captions, leading to weak cross-modal alignment. To address these issues, we propose BPA-Net, a framework for bidirectional prototype alignment. Specifically, the Dual Prototype Extraction module independently projects visual features and ground-truth text tokens into a shared semantic space to form structured prototypes. Then, the Cross-Modal Prototype Alignment module enables bidirectional interaction and alignment between visual and textual prototypes guided by symmetric matching objectives. This approach effectively mitigates the noise inherent in external proxy texts and establishes robust semantic alignment. Extensive experiments on the MS-COCO, Flickr8k, and Flickr30k benchmarks demonstrate that BPA-Net achieves state-of-the-art performance. Furthermore, evaluations on the Nocaps dataset confirm its strong generalization capability for describing novel objects. The code is available at https://github.com/CarasuLee/BPA-Net.

item.title: Relative Depth Guided Cross Domain Semantic Segmentation for High Resolution Satellite Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Yongqi Sun
Yujun Quan
Anzhu Yu
Yu Su
Xin Li
Chenguang Dai
Xuanguang Liu
Yanfei Zhong
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Satellite remote sensing images exhibit significant cross-domain discrepancies due to variations in sensors, imaging conditions, and regional characteristics. These discrepancies degrade the performance of semantic segmentation models on unseen target domains. Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) mitigates this problem but requires target domain images during training. Achieving robust cross-domain generalization with foundation models typically relies on large-scale architectures, incurring substantial computational overhead that limits practical deployment. This paper introduces a novel perspective by leveraging zero-shot relative depth maps as domain-invariant geometric priors to guide cross-domain semantic segmentation of satellite remote sensing images. The proposed framework is compatible with most encoder-decoder architectures and makes three key contributions: 1) A unified depth-guided framework for optical remote sensing cross-domain segmentation. 2) A multi-scale contrastive learning module that aligns optical and relative depth features, enhancing optical feature representation. 3) A refinement strategy using shallow relative depth features to guide semantic mask prediction. Experiments across three cross-domain scenarios, including the LoveDA benchmark, a self-constructed circumpolar cross-seasonal dataset, and a cross=-dataset generalization setting, demonstrate that relative depth serves as an effective spatial prior. Notably, the proposed method achieves comparable or superior accuracy to state-of-the-art UDA approaches that require target-domain images during training, while operating under the stricter zero-shot domain generalization setting where no target data is accessible. The codes will be released at https://github.com/ArthasMil/DGCD. Published: 2026-06-09T19:52:19+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.807 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yongqi Sun; Yujun Quan; Anzhu Yu; Yu Su; Xin Li; Chenguang Dai; Xuanguang Liu; Yanfei Zhong

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3701909

Relevance: 0.807 (must_read)

Satellite remote sensing images exhibit significant cross-domain discrepancies due to variations in sensors, imaging conditions, and regional characteristics. These discrepancies degrade the performance of semantic segmentation models on unseen target domains. Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) mitigates this problem but requires target domain images during training. Achieving robust cross-domain generalization with foundation models typically relies on large-scale architectures, incurring substantial computational overhead that limits practical deployment. This paper introduces a novel perspective by leveraging zero-shot relative depth maps as domain-invariant geometric priors to guide cross-domain semantic segmentation of satellite remote sensing images. The proposed framework is compatible with most encoder-decoder architectures and makes three key contributions: 1) A unified depth-guided framework for optical remote sensing cross-domain segmentation. 2) A multi-scale contrastive learning module that aligns optical and relative depth features, enhancing optical feature representation. 3) A refinement strategy using shallow relative depth features to guide semantic mask prediction. Experiments across three cross-domain scenarios, including the LoveDA benchmark, a self-constructed circumpolar cross-seasonal dataset, and a cross=-dataset generalization setting, demonstrate that relative depth serves as an effective spatial prior. Notably, the proposed method achieves comparable or superior accuracy to state-of-the-art UDA approaches that require target-domain images during training, while operating under the stricter zero-shot domain generalization setting where no target data is accessible. The codes will be released at https://github.com/ArthasMil/DGCD.

item.title: Infrared Small Target Detection via Graph Fourier Transform Tensor Nuclear Norm and Spatio-Temporal Overlapping Group Sparsity
item.dc:creator: Anqing Wu
Qiang Guo
Mykola Kaliuzhnyi
Yupeng Chen
Na Fu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Infrared small target detection in heterogeneous and dynamic scenes remains a challenging problem due to the lack of intrinsic target features and the complexity of background clutter. Although Tensor Robust Principal Component Analysis (TRPCA) methods have achieved significant progress, they typically rely on the singular value decomposition (SVD) based tensor nuclear norm (TNN), which assumes linear correlations across temporal frames. This assumption often fails in highly dynamic scenes (e.g., rapid cloud movement or sensor jitter) where background changes are non-linear. To address this limitation, this article proposes a novel method combining Graph Fourier Transform-based Tensor Nuclear Norm (GFT-TNN) and Spatio-Temporal Overlapping Group Sparsity (ST-OGS). First, to capture the intrinsic manifold structure of dynamic backgrounds, we construct a temporal graph that encodes the non-linear relationships between frames. A GFT-TNN is then introduced to constrain the background tensor in the graph spectral domain, offering a more robust low-rank representation than traditional SVD-based approaches. Second, recognizing that targets exhibit spatio-temporal continuity rather than appearing as isolated pixels, we employ an ST-OGS regularization. This imposes a 3D structural constraint on the target tensor, effectively distinguishing true targets from random, high-intensity sensor noise. Finally, to solve the proposed model effectively, we develop an optimization algorithm based on the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM). Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method yields superior Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) and background suppression capabilities compared to state-of-the-art baselines. Published: 2026-06-09T19:52:19+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Anqing Wu; Qiang Guo; Mykola Kaliuzhnyi; Yupeng Chen; Na Fu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3701330

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Infrared small target detection in heterogeneous and dynamic scenes remains a challenging problem due to the lack of intrinsic target features and the complexity of background clutter. Although Tensor Robust Principal Component Analysis (TRPCA) methods have achieved significant progress, they typically rely on the singular value decomposition (SVD) based tensor nuclear norm (TNN), which assumes linear correlations across temporal frames. This assumption often fails in highly dynamic scenes (e.g., rapid cloud movement or sensor jitter) where background changes are non-linear. To address this limitation, this article proposes a novel method combining Graph Fourier Transform-based Tensor Nuclear Norm (GFT-TNN) and Spatio-Temporal Overlapping Group Sparsity (ST-OGS). First, to capture the intrinsic manifold structure of dynamic backgrounds, we construct a temporal graph that encodes the non-linear relationships between frames. A GFT-TNN is then introduced to constrain the background tensor in the graph spectral domain, offering a more robust low-rank representation than traditional SVD-based approaches. Second, recognizing that targets exhibit spatio-temporal continuity rather than appearing as isolated pixels, we employ an ST-OGS regularization. This imposes a 3D structural constraint on the target tensor, effectively distinguishing true targets from random, high-intensity sensor noise. Finally, to solve the proposed model effectively, we develop an optimization algorithm based on the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM). Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method yields superior Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) and background suppression capabilities compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

item.title: An RGB-Event Hierarchical Fusion Enhancement Network for Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Kaiyi Wang
Xinyu Hou
Siying Liu
Chen Cheng
Lei Deng
Hao Guo
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Object detection in frame-based vision systems often faces the problem of significant performance degradation under challenging conditions due to the inherent sensing limitations of conventional cameras. Event cameras, with their advantages of high temporal resolution and sparse data output, offer a promising solution for addressing the performance degradation problem. However, due to the asynchronous and sparse nature of event data, event camera–based detection methods generally exhibit lower accuracy than their image-based counterparts. Therefore, effectively fusing data from both event and frame-based cameras represents the main challenge. To this end, this study introduces a hierarchical fusion enhancement network (FENET) designed to achieve more robust object detection through complementary multimodal fusion. First, to address the problem of underutilization of event timescales in conventional dense representations, this study designs an innovative, learnable multi-timescale event preprocessing module. This module generates temporal aggregation frames (TAFs) by leveraging event data from a spectrum of intervals, ranging from standard RGB exposure times down to finer timescales. This approach ensures the comprehensive capture and clear delineation of the target's structure, thereby enhancing the integrity of the visual information. Furthermore, a two-branch adaptive enhanced cross-modal aggregation module (ECAM), which guides the adaptive fusion of features from both modalities by first self-enhancing the features of each, is employed. The main goal of the ECAM is to achieve efficient feature aggregation by combining global context with local spatial information, subsequently using the aggregated features to steer the adaptive fusion process. Finally, by recognizing the scarcity of large-scale, high-quality annotated datasets for event-based object detection, the DSEC-D dataset comprising 60 sequences, with a total of 70,739 images and over 250,000 labels, is ma... Published: 2026-06-10T20:04:43+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.800 (must_read)

The main goal of the ECAM is to achieve efficient feature aggregation by combining global context with local spatial information, subsequently using the aggregated features to steer the adaptive fusion process. Finally, by recognizing the scarcity of large-scale, high-quality annotated datasets for event-based object detection, the DSEC-D dataset comprising 60 sequences, with a total of 70,739 images and over 250,000 labels, is ma...

item.title: Navigating molecular OOD-ness
item.dc:creator: Anna Tomberg
Nessa Carson
item.prism:publicationName: Nature Machine Intelligence
item.description: Machine learning methods for drug discovery often face difficulties in identifying novel bioactive molecules that do not belong to the training data distribution. A new metric can now quantify chemical distribution shift and evaluate the generalization capability of molecular machine learning models. Published: 2026-06-09T09:02:09+00:00 Venue: Nature Machine Intelligence Score: 0.799 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Anna Tomberg; Nessa Carson

Journal: Nature Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1038/s42256-026-01251-7

Relevance: 0.799 (must_read)

Machine learning methods for drug discovery often face difficulties in identifying novel bioactive molecules that do not belong to the training data distribution. A new metric can now quantify chemical distribution shift and evaluate the generalization capability of molecular machine learning models.

item.title: Selection, Aggregation, and Enhancement: Trajectory Consistent Diffusion Model for Image Super-Resolution
item.dc:creator: Detian Huang
Yaohui Guo
Yuting Huang
Luanyuan Dai
Fei Shen
Huanqiang Zeng
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
item.description: Diffusion models have shown strong promise for image super-resolution (ISR). However, current approaches often underuse pretrained diffusion backbones and lack constraints on the sampling trajectory, which degrades structural consistency and fine details. For that, we introduce the trajectory consistent diffusion model (TCDM) for super-resolution, which jointly optimizes the sampling process through lightweight components and inference-time strategies while keeping the diffusion backbone frozen, yielding high-fidelity, detail-rich reconstructions. First, we propose a dynamic semantic selection (DSS) mechanism that records early intermediates, matches them to upsampled low-resolution features, and reconditions sampling with the best match to reduce the mismatch between conditioning and noise scale. Next, we design a cross-step aggregation guidance (CAG) strategy that aggregates features from the current state with the selected intermediate to enforce trajectory-level consistency in noise prediction. Finally, we present a plug-and-play frequency enhancement adapter (FE-Adapter) that injects different frequency-domain cues into the encoder during training, strengthening high-frequency perception while preserving global structures. Extensive experiments on multiple ISR benchmarks show that TCDM achieves strong structural fidelity and competitive no-reference perceptual quality, offering a favorable fidelity–perception trade-off. Published: 2026-06-10T20:05:16+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.797 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Detian Huang; Yaohui Guo; Yuting Huang; Luanyuan Dai; Fei Shen; Huanqiang Zeng

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3699085

Relevance: 0.797 (must_read)

Diffusion models have shown strong promise for image super-resolution (ISR). However, current approaches often underuse pretrained diffusion backbones and lack constraints on the sampling trajectory, which degrades structural consistency and fine details. For that, we introduce the trajectory consistent diffusion model (TCDM) for super-resolution, which jointly optimizes the sampling process through lightweight components and inference-time strategies while keeping the diffusion backbone frozen, yielding high-fidelity, detail-rich reconstructions. First, we propose a dynamic semantic selection (DSS) mechanism that records early intermediates, matches them to upsampled low-resolution features, and reconditions sampling with the best match to reduce the mismatch between conditioning and noise scale. Next, we design a cross-step aggregation guidance (CAG) strategy that aggregates features from the current state with the selected intermediate to enforce trajectory-level consistency in noise prediction. Finally, we present a plug-and-play frequency enhancement adapter (FE-Adapter) that injects different frequency-domain cues into the encoder during training, strengthening high-frequency perception while preserving global structures. Extensive experiments on multiple ISR benchmarks show that TCDM achieves strong structural fidelity and competitive no-reference perceptual quality, offering a favorable fidelity–perception trade-off.

item.title: Reinforcing Out-of-Distribution SAR Ship Detection: A Guidance-Constraint Hybrid Approach
item.dc:creator: Qiansheng Ma
Zhe Chen
Yun Zhang
Zhiquan Ding
Qinglong Hua
Zhenxi Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization remains a critical challenge in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) ship detection, where training and test data often originate from different distributions. Conventional approaches such as Domain Generalization (DG) and Domain Adaptation (DA) rely heavily on data diversity, limiting their efficacy in real-world scenarios with limited training data. To address this, we propose a guidance-constraint hybrid framework that integrates structured knowledge into data-driven detection pipeline. Candidate regions are first identified using a classical local Constant False Alarm Rate (CFAR) detector, serving as guidance. Confidence estimation is then refined via {2^{{\mathrm{{nd}}}}} {2^{{\mathrm{{nd}}}}} order Gaussian filtration, acting as a constraint to suppress noise and enhance spatial coherence. The data-driven branch employs Enhanced ShuffleNet v2 (ESv2), a lightweight architecture designed to improve semantic correlation and attention to relevant regions. Experimental results demonstrate that our hybrid model achieves robust performance under severe distributional shifts, with an absolute improvement of approximately 20% in mAP@0.5 on OOD scenarios compared to conventional data-driven methods. These findings highlight the potential of informed machine learning to complement statistical approaches and advance strict OOD generalization in SAR ship detection. Published: 2026-06-09T19:52:54+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.796 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qiansheng Ma; Zhe Chen; Yun Zhang; Zhiquan Ding; Qinglong Hua; Zhenxi Zhang

Journal: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/jstars.2026.3701929

Relevance: 0.796 (must_read)

Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization remains a critical challenge in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) ship detection, where training and test data often originate from different distributions. Conventional approaches such as Domain Generalization (DG) and Domain Adaptation (DA) rely heavily on data diversity, limiting their efficacy in real-world scenarios with limited training data. To address this, we propose a guidance-constraint hybrid framework that integrates structured knowledge into data-driven detection pipeline. Candidate regions are first identified using a classical local Constant False Alarm Rate (CFAR) detector, serving as guidance. Confidence estimation is then refined via {2^{{\mathrm{{nd}}}}} {2^{{\mathrm{{nd}}}}} order Gaussian filtration, acting as a constraint to suppress noise and enhance spatial coherence. The data-driven branch employs Enhanced ShuffleNet v2 (ESv2), a lightweight architecture designed to improve semantic correlation and attention to relevant regions. Experimental results demonstrate that our hybrid model achieves robust performance under severe distributional shifts, with an absolute improvement of approximately 20% in mAP@0.5 on OOD scenarios compared to conventional data-driven methods. These findings highlight the potential of informed machine learning to complement statistical approaches and advance strict OOD generalization in SAR ship detection.

item.title: Efficient Inference for Large Reasoning Models: A Survey
item.dc:creator: Yue Liu
Jiaying Wu
Yufei He
Ruihan Gong
Jun Xia
Liang Li
Hongcheng Gao
Hongyu Chen
Baolong Bi
Jiaheng Zhang
Zhiqi Huang
Bryan Hooi
Stan Z. Li
Keqin Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
item.description: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) significantly improve the reasoning ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) by learning to reason, exhibiting promising performance in solving complex tasks. However, their deliberative reasoning process leads to inefficiencies in token usage, memory consumption, and inference time. Thus, this survey provides a review of efficient inference methods designed specifically for LRMs, focusing on mitigating token inefficiency while preserving the reasoning quality. The overview structure of this paper is shown in Figure 1. First, we introduce a taxonomy to group the recent methods into two main categories: (a) explicit compact Chain-of-Thought (CoT), which reduces tokens while keeping the explicit reasoning structure, and (b) implicit latent CoT, which encodes reasoning steps within hidden representations instead of explicit tokens. Meanwhile, we discuss their strengths and weaknesses. Then, we conduct empirical analyses on existing methods from reasoning scenarios, object functions, and performance & efficiency aspects. Besides, we present open challenges in this field, including human-centric controllable reasoning, trade-off between interpretability and efficiency of reasoning, ensuring the safety of efficient reasoning, and broader applications of efficient reasoning. In addition, we highlight key insights for enhancing LRMs' inference efficiency via techniques such as model merging, new architectures, and agent routers. We hope this work serves as a valuable guide, helping researchers overcome challenges in this vibrant field. A collection of efficient reasoning methods for LRMs (papers and codes) is provided at this link: https://github.com/yueliu1999/Awesome-Efficient-Inference-for-LRMs. Published: 2026-06-09T19:52:16+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence Score: 0.796 (must_read)

Authors: Yue Liu; Jiaying Wu; Yufei He; Ruihan Gong; Jun Xia; Liang Li; Hongcheng Gao; Hongyu Chen; Baolong Bi; Jiaheng Zhang; Zhiqi Huang; Bryan Hooi; Stan Z. Li; Keqin Li

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3701928

Relevance: 0.796 (must_read)

Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) significantly improve the reasoning ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) by learning to reason, exhibiting promising performance in solving complex tasks. However, their deliberative reasoning process leads to inefficiencies in token usage, memory consumption, and inference time. Thus, this survey provides a review of efficient inference methods designed specifically for LRMs, focusing on mitigating token inefficiency while preserving the reasoning quality. The overview structure of this paper is shown in Figure 1. First, we introduce a taxonomy to group the recent methods into two main categories: (a) explicit compact Chain-of-Thought (CoT), which reduces tokens while keeping the explicit reasoning structure, and (b) implicit latent CoT, which encodes reasoning steps within hidden representations instead of explicit tokens. Meanwhile, we discuss their strengths and weaknesses. Then, we conduct empirical analyses on existing methods from reasoning scenarios, object functions, and performance & efficiency aspects. Besides, we present open challenges in this field, including human-centric controllable reasoning, trade-off between interpretability and efficiency of reasoning, ensuring the safety of efficient reasoning, and broader applications of efficient reasoning. In addition, we highlight key insights for enhancing LRMs' inference efficiency via techniques such as model merging, new architectures, and agent routers. We hope this work serves as a valuable guide, helping researchers overcome challenges in this vibrant field. A collection of efficient reasoning methods for LRMs (papers and codes) is provided at this link: https://github.com/yueliu1999/Awesome-Efficient-Inference-for-LRMs.

item.title: A Glimpse to Compress: Dynamic Visual Token Pruning for Large Vision-Language Models
item.dc:creator: Quan-Sheng Zeng
Yunheng Li
Qilong Wang
Peng-Tao Jiang
Zuxuan Wu
Ming-Ming Cheng
Qibin Hou
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Visual token compression is critical for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to efficiently process high-resolution inputs. Existing methods that typically adopt fixed compression ratios cannot adapt to scenes of varying complexity, often causing imprecise pruning that discards informative visual tokens and results in degraded model performance. To address this issue, we introduce a dynamic pruning framework, GlimpsePrune, inspired by human cognition. It takes a data-driven “glimpse” and prunes irrelevant visual tokens in a single forward pass before answer generation. This approach prunes 92.4% of visual tokens while on average nearly preserving the original performance on free-form VQA tasks, causing end-to-end latency and peak memory to reduce to 66% and 67% of the baseline, respectively. By integrating our pruning mechanism into the reinforcement learning fine-tuning stage, the time and memory costs of fine-tuning are also reduced with negligible performance loss. Code is available at https: //github.com/HVision-NKU/GlimpsePrune. Published: 2026-06-10T20:04:43+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.796 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Quan-Sheng Zeng; Yunheng Li; Qilong Wang; Peng-Tao Jiang; Zuxuan Wu; Ming-Ming Cheng; Qibin Hou

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3702147

Relevance: 0.796 (must_read)

Visual token compression is critical for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to efficiently process high-resolution inputs. Existing methods that typically adopt fixed compression ratios cannot adapt to scenes of varying complexity, often causing imprecise pruning that discards informative visual tokens and results in degraded model performance. To address this issue, we introduce a dynamic pruning framework, GlimpsePrune, inspired by human cognition. It takes a data-driven “glimpse” and prunes irrelevant visual tokens in a single forward pass before answer generation. This approach prunes 92.4% of visual tokens while on average nearly preserving the original performance on free-form VQA tasks, causing end-to-end latency and peak memory to reduce to 66% and 67% of the baseline, respectively. By integrating our pruning mechanism into the reinforcement learning fine-tuning stage, the time and memory costs of fine-tuning are also reduced with negligible performance loss. Code is available at https: //github.com/HVision-NKU/GlimpsePrune.

item.title: VQFusion: Infrared and Visible Image Fusion via Codebook Prior Guidance
item.dc:creator: Cong Bi
Xue Wang
Wenhua Qian
Kaixiang Yan
item.prism:publicationName: Information Fusion
item.description: Current infrared and visible image fusion methods often struggle to handle degradations such as noise, low contrast, blur, and adverse weather conditions, leading to the loss of fine texture details and insufficient target saliency. To address these issues, we propose VQFusion, a codebook prior-guided infrared and visible image fusion method for degraded scenarios. The proposed method consists of two stages. In the first stage, we train a vector-quantized generative adversarial network (VQGAN) to learn a semantically rich codebook prior. In the second stage, we design a feature alignment mechanism to align degraded latent features with the learned codebook distribution, thereby enabling robust quantization. To mitigate information loss during feature alignment, we further design a detail compensation module to integrate original features with quantized representations, effectively preserving fine-grained structural details. Furthermore, a bidirectional cross-attention mechanism is designed to facilitate deep cross-modal feature interaction and enhance complementary information extraction. Extensive experiments across various degradation scenarios demonstrate that VQFusion outperforms nine state-of-the-art methods in both visual quality and quantitative metrics. On the LLVIP dataset, VQFusion achieves mutual information (MI) and visual information fidelity (VIF) scores of 2.470 and 1.130, respectively, representing improvements of 20.3% and 29.0% over URFusion. Published: 2026-06-09T16:41:07+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.796 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Cong Bi; Xue Wang; Wenhua Qian; Kaixiang Yan

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104544

Relevance: 0.796 (must_read)

Current infrared and visible image fusion methods often struggle to handle degradations such as noise, low contrast, blur, and adverse weather conditions, leading to the loss of fine texture details and insufficient target saliency. To address these issues, we propose VQFusion, a codebook prior-guided infrared and visible image fusion method for degraded scenarios. The proposed method consists of two stages. In the first stage, we train a vector-quantized generative adversarial network (VQGAN) to learn a semantically rich codebook prior. In the second stage, we design a feature alignment mechanism to align degraded latent features with the learned codebook distribution, thereby enabling robust quantization. To mitigate information loss during feature alignment, we further design a detail compensation module to integrate original features with quantized representations, effectively preserving fine-grained structural details. Furthermore, a bidirectional cross-attention mechanism is designed to facilitate deep cross-modal feature interaction and enhance complementary information extraction. Extensive experiments across various degradation scenarios demonstrate that VQFusion outperforms nine state-of-the-art methods in both visual quality and quantitative metrics. On the LLVIP dataset, VQFusion achieves mutual information (MI) and visual information fidelity (VIF) scores of 2.470 and 1.130, respectively, representing improvements of 20.3% and 29.0% over URFusion.

item.title: Diffusion-based Data Augmentation and Knowledge Distillation with Generated Soft Labels for Oil Spill Segmentation Learning on Satellite SAR Images
item.dc:creator: Jaeho Moon
Jeonghwan Yun
Jaehyun Kim
Jaehyup Lee
Munchurl Kim
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Semantic segmentation often suffers from a compounded scarcity of data, where both input observations and ground-truth labels are limited. This problem is particularly severe in remote sensing tasks such as oil spill segmentation using Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), where rare event occurrence and the difficulty of SAR image acquisition result in limited training data. To address this limitation, we propose a diffusion-based Data Augmentation with Knowledge Transfer (DAKTer) strategy. Our DAKTer strategy enables a diffusion model to generate SAR oil spill images along with soft label pairs, which offer richer class probability distributions than segmentation masks (i.e. hard labels). Also, for reliable joint generation of high-quality SAR images and soft labels with high correspondence, we introduce an SNR-based balancing factor aligning the noise corruption process of both modalities in diffusion models. By leveraging the generated SAR images and soft labels, a student segmentation model can learn robust feature representations without teacher models trained for the same segmentation task, improving its ability to segment oil spill regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DAKTer strategy effectively transfers the knowledge of per-pixel class probabilities to the student segmentation model to distinguish the oil spill regions from other look-alike regions in the SAR images. Our DAKTer strategy boosts various segmentation models to achieve superior performance with large margins compared to other generative data augmentation methods. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:00+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.795 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jaeho Moon; Jeonghwan Yun; Jaehyun Kim; Jaehyup Lee; Munchurl Kim

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3701691

Relevance: 0.795 (must_read)

Semantic segmentation often suffers from a compounded scarcity of data, where both input observations and ground-truth labels are limited. This problem is particularly severe in remote sensing tasks such as oil spill segmentation using Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), where rare event occurrence and the difficulty of SAR image acquisition result in limited training data. To address this limitation, we propose a diffusion-based Data Augmentation with Knowledge Transfer (DAKTer) strategy. Our DAKTer strategy enables a diffusion model to generate SAR oil spill images along with soft label pairs, which offer richer class probability distributions than segmentation masks (i.e. hard labels). Also, for reliable joint generation of high-quality SAR images and soft labels with high correspondence, we introduce an SNR-based balancing factor aligning the noise corruption process of both modalities in diffusion models. By leveraging the generated SAR images and soft labels, a student segmentation model can learn robust feature representations without teacher models trained for the same segmentation task, improving its ability to segment oil spill regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DAKTer strategy effectively transfers the knowledge of per-pixel class probabilities to the student segmentation model to distinguish the oil spill regions from other look-alike regions in the SAR images. Our DAKTer strategy boosts various segmentation models to achieve superior performance with large margins compared to other generative data augmentation methods.

item.title: Earth-OneVision: Extending Remote Sensing Multimodal Large Language Models to More Sensor Modalities and Tasks
item.dc:creator: Miaoxin Cai
Guanqun Wang
Wei Zhang
Guangyao Zhou
Yin Zhuang
Tong Zhang
Hao Wang
He Chen
Jun Li
item.prism:publicationName: arXiv
item.description: RS-MLLMs enable natural-language understanding and spatial reasoning over earth observation imagery. However, existing models support only a narrow range of sensor types and tasks, yielding a fragmented view of the earth and leaving cross-modal geoscientific knowledge largely unexploited. This work presents Earth-OneVision, a 2B RS-MLLM that unifies six sensor modalities (i.e., optical, SAR, infrared, multispectral, temporal, and video) and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories within a single autoregressive framework. Three dedicated mechanisms address three bottlenecks. Full-Granularity Vision-Language Alignment (FGVLA) aligns multi-level visual features with the multi-dimensional language space. Spatial-Linguistic Isomorphic Serialization (SLIS) unifies heterogeneous spatial outputs as autoregressive tokens. Progressive Cross-Modality Adaptation (PCMA) decomposes the compound domain gap into sequential stages, tackling the viewpoint and imaging physics gaps in turn. To support joint training, MMRS-OneVision is constructed with ~34M QA pairs spanning all six sensor modalities and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories, substantially exceeding existing RS multimodal instruction datasets. With only 2B parameters, Earth-OneVision achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results across extensive benchmarks, consistently matching or outperforming 4B-72B RS-MLLMs. It achieves 87.52% P@0.5 on the OPT-RSVG testset for optical visual grounding and 80.68% on the SAR VQA benchmark SARLANG-Bench, exceeding 7B models by over 7%. It further achieves 75.74% recall on the BigEarthNet-MS testset for multispectral classification, and 81.94% MCQ accuracy on EarthMind-Bench for cross-modality reasoning. Published: 2026-06-09T13:01:51+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.794 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Miaoxin Cai; Guanqun Wang; Wei Zhang; Guangyao Zhou; Yin Zhuang; Tong Zhang; Hao Wang; He Chen; Jun Li

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.794 (must_read)

RS-MLLMs enable natural-language understanding and spatial reasoning over earth observation imagery. However, existing models support only a narrow range of sensor types and tasks, yielding a fragmented view of the earth and leaving cross-modal geoscientific knowledge largely unexploited. This work presents Earth-OneVision, a 2B RS-MLLM that unifies six sensor modalities (i.e., optical, SAR, infrared, multispectral, temporal, and video) and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories within a single autoregressive framework. Three dedicated mechanisms address three bottlenecks. Full-Granularity Vision-Language Alignment (FGVLA) aligns multi-level visual features with the multi-dimensional language space. Spatial-Linguistic Isomorphic Serialization (SLIS) unifies heterogeneous spatial outputs as autoregressive tokens. Progressive Cross-Modality Adaptation (PCMA) decomposes the compound domain gap into sequential stages, tackling the viewpoint and imaging physics gaps in turn. To support joint training, MMRS-OneVision is constructed with ~34M QA pairs spanning all six sensor modalities and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories, substantially exceeding existing RS multimodal instruction datasets. With only 2B parameters, Earth-OneVision achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results across extensive benchmarks, consistently matching or outperforming 4B-72B RS-MLLMs. It achieves 87.52% P@0.5 on the OPT-RSVG testset for optical visual grounding and 80.68% on the SAR VQA benchmark SARLANG-Bench, exceeding 7B models by over 7%. It further achieves 75.74% recall on the BigEarthNet-MS testset for multispectral classification, and 81.94% MCQ accuracy on EarthMind-Bench for cross-modality reasoning.

item.title: DIAI-net: An Integrated Network for Deep SAR Imaging and Autofocusing
item.dc:creator: Wei Pu
Yue Song
Chao Zhou
Xiaoting Wang
Junjie Wu
Yulin Huang
Haiguang Yang
Jianyu Yang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: With the increasing demand for high-precision imaging, synthetic aperture radar (SAR) has seen increasingly widespread applications in the field of remote sensing, and deep learning SAR imaging net has achieved fruitful developments. However, due to deviations in the trajectory, motion errors are present in the actual SAR echoes. Therefore, developing an integrated network for imaging and auto-focus to obtain high-precision SAR images holds meaningful importance. To address this issue, this paper proposes an integrated SAR imaging and autofocus strategy, i.e., (DIAI-net). The proposed DIAI-net is composed of two sub-networks, i.e., Image Reconstruction based on deep SAR imaging prior network (IrDIP) and Measurement matrix Estimation based on deep SAR imaging prior network (MeDIP). In both IrDIP and MeDIP, unrolled Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) network architecture is applied to exploit prior information. Testing results on both simulated and real SAR data demonstrate that the proposed DIAI-net leads to more accurate estimation of measurement matrix and better imaging results compared with the existing SAR auto-focus techniques. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:15+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.794 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wei Pu; Yue Song; Chao Zhou; Xiaoting Wang; Junjie Wu; Yulin Huang; Haiguang Yang; Jianyu Yang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3701857

Relevance: 0.794 (must_read)

With the increasing demand for high-precision imaging, synthetic aperture radar (SAR) has seen increasingly widespread applications in the field of remote sensing, and deep learning SAR imaging net has achieved fruitful developments. However, due to deviations in the trajectory, motion errors are present in the actual SAR echoes. Therefore, developing an integrated network for imaging and auto-focus to obtain high-precision SAR images holds meaningful importance. To address this issue, this paper proposes an integrated SAR imaging and autofocus strategy, i.e., (DIAI-net). The proposed DIAI-net is composed of two sub-networks, i.e., Image Reconstruction based on deep SAR imaging prior network (IrDIP) and Measurement matrix Estimation based on deep SAR imaging prior network (MeDIP). In both IrDIP and MeDIP, unrolled Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) network architecture is applied to exploit prior information. Testing results on both simulated and real SAR data demonstrate that the proposed DIAI-net leads to more accurate estimation of measurement matrix and better imaging results compared with the existing SAR auto-focus techniques.

item.title: Stage-Wise Feature-Guided Imbalance Diffusion Model for Imbalanced Semi-Supervised SAR Target Recognition
item.dc:creator: Xinzheng Zhang
Guopeng Li
Yuqing Luo
Dong Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery plays a key role in various defense and civilian applications. While recent semi-supervised learning methods have advanced SAR ATR, they often struggle when training data is class-imbalanced-a common scenario in practice. To address this challenge, we propose a method that combines class-rebalancing with self-training. Our approach consists of two main parts: a sample synthesis module and a semi-supervised learning module. The synthesis module uses a specially designed diffusion model to generate realistic samples for minority classes, guided by both global features and scattering center features in a stage-wise manner. The semi-supervised module introduces a Bayesian-rebalancing energy score to improve pseudo-label screening, reducing bias toward majority classes. Together, these components help the model learn a more balanced and accurate classifier. Experiments on highly imbalanced SAR datasets show that our method significantly improves recognition performance and reduces class imbalance effects. Published: 2026-06-09T19:55:15+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.793 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xinzheng Zhang; Guopeng Li; Yuqing Luo; Dong Li

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3701860

Relevance: 0.793 (must_read)

Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery plays a key role in various defense and civilian applications. While recent semi-supervised learning methods have advanced SAR ATR, they often struggle when training data is class-imbalanced-a common scenario in practice. To address this challenge, we propose a method that combines class-rebalancing with self-training. Our approach consists of two main parts: a sample synthesis module and a semi-supervised learning module. The synthesis module uses a specially designed diffusion model to generate realistic samples for minority classes, guided by both global features and scattering center features in a stage-wise manner. The semi-supervised module introduces a Bayesian-rebalancing energy score to improve pseudo-label screening, reducing bias toward majority classes. Together, these components help the model learn a more balanced and accurate classifier. Experiments on highly imbalanced SAR datasets show that our method significantly improves recognition performance and reduces class imbalance effects.

item.title: Multi-Scale Symmetric Difference Attention-Based Transformer for Panchromatic and Multispectral Classification
item.dc:creator: Yuantian Zhu
Hao Zhu
Zeyu Lei
Xiaotong Li
Huihui Dong
Biao Hou
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Nowadays, effectively exploiting multispectral (MS) and panchromatic (PAN) imagery for their joint classification task has attracted wide attention. However, due to their heterogeneous resolutions and distinct modality characteristics, the precise alignment of different-scale semantic features and the compact fusion of modality-specific information remain challenging. To tackle these issues, we propose a multi-scale symmetric difference attention-based Transformer (MSAFormer). It comprises two key modules: texture-aware multi-scale feature generation (TMFG) module and symmetric difference feature divergence interaction (SDFD) module. By exploiting PAN’s high-resolution texture to bridge the inherent texture deficiency of MS at continuous resolutions, TMFG builds a multi-scale space with precise texture alignment. This module constructs a robust foundation for the comprehensive extraction of dual-source specific features during interaction. Subsequently, by applying the concept of symmetric difference between sets to the aligned dual-modality groups of different scales, SDFD generates compact and comprehensive multi-scale fusion representations in a non-decoupled manner. These lightweight fusion features integrate the enhanced modality-specific features and eliminate the overlapped redundancy of shared information, ultimately yielding superior classification performance. Several experimental results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the network. Codes are available at https://github.com/Xidian-AIGroup190726/MSAformer. Published: 2026-06-09T19:52:19+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.793 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuantian Zhu; Hao Zhu; Zeyu Lei; Xiaotong Li; Huihui Dong; Biao Hou

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3701412

Relevance: 0.793 (must_read)

Nowadays, effectively exploiting multispectral (MS) and panchromatic (PAN) imagery for their joint classification task has attracted wide attention. However, due to their heterogeneous resolutions and distinct modality characteristics, the precise alignment of different-scale semantic features and the compact fusion of modality-specific information remain challenging. To tackle these issues, we propose a multi-scale symmetric difference attention-based Transformer (MSAFormer). It comprises two key modules: texture-aware multi-scale feature generation (TMFG) module and symmetric difference feature divergence interaction (SDFD) module. By exploiting PAN’s high-resolution texture to bridge the inherent texture deficiency of MS at continuous resolutions, TMFG builds a multi-scale space with precise texture alignment. This module constructs a robust foundation for the comprehensive extraction of dual-source specific features during interaction. Subsequently, by applying the concept of symmetric difference between sets to the aligned dual-modality groups of different scales, SDFD generates compact and comprehensive multi-scale fusion representations in a non-decoupled manner. These lightweight fusion features integrate the enhanced modality-specific features and eliminate the overlapped redundancy of shared information, ultimately yielding superior classification performance. Several experimental results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the network. Codes are available at https://github.com/Xidian-AIGroup190726/MSAformer.

item.title: Few-shot classification across heterogeneous tasks: A dual-level meta-learning framework with graph-based alignment and modulation
item.dc:creator: Maofa Wang
Xinru Li
Zhixiong Leng
Runping Ma
Xiao Zheng
Huiling Chen
item.prism:publicationName: Expert Systems with Applications
item.description: Meta-learning seeks to enable rapid adaptation to new tasks by leveraging shared knowledge across tasks. However, existing optimization-based meta-learning methods often overfit to task-specific features when confronted with task heterogeneity arising from multimodal task distributions, thereby limiting cross-task generalization performance. This paper proposes Meta-learning with Graph-based Alignment and Modulation (MGAM), a novel plug-and-play dual-level framework primarily designed for optimization-based meta-learning and aimed at alleviating feature distribution discrepancies at both the intra-task and inter-task levels. Graph-based Task Alignment (GTA) explicitly models inter-task similarities and constructs a task graph to generate cross-task collective perception, while using regularization to promote task-level alignment and reduce potential bias introduced by single-teacher distillation. Meta Semantic Modulator (MSM) partitions input features into structured semantic clusters at the feature level and adaptively modulates the activation strengths of semantic clusters across tasks in the outer-loop, thereby alleviating the burden of rapid adaptation in the inner-loop and mitigating intra-task feature distribution inconsistencies. Extensive experiments show that MGAM significantly improves the performance of representative optimization-based meta-learning methods on standard few-shot classification, fine-grained classification, and a real-world rock photomicrograph dataset, demonstrating robust cross-task alignment and rapid adaptation capabilities. Published: 2026-06-10T15:29:35+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Maofa Wang; Xinru Li; Zhixiong Leng; Runping Ma; Xiao Zheng; Huiling Chen

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.133232

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Meta-learning seeks to enable rapid adaptation to new tasks by leveraging shared knowledge across tasks. However, existing optimization-based meta-learning methods often overfit to task-specific features when confronted with task heterogeneity arising from multimodal task distributions, thereby limiting cross-task generalization performance. This paper proposes Meta-learning with Graph-based Alignment and Modulation (MGAM), a novel plug-and-play dual-level framework primarily designed for optimization-based meta-learning and aimed at alleviating feature distribution discrepancies at both the intra-task and inter-task levels. Graph-based Task Alignment (GTA) explicitly models inter-task similarities and constructs a task graph to generate cross-task collective perception, while using regularization to promote task-level alignment and reduce potential bias introduced by single-teacher distillation. Meta Semantic Modulator (MSM) partitions input features into structured semantic clusters at the feature level and adaptively modulates the activation strengths of semantic clusters across tasks in the outer-loop, thereby alleviating the burden of rapid adaptation in the inner-loop and mitigating intra-task feature distribution inconsistencies. Extensive experiments show that MGAM significantly improves the performance of representative optimization-based meta-learning methods on standard few-shot classification, fine-grained classification, and a real-world rock photomicrograph dataset, demonstrating robust cross-task alignment and rapid adaptation capabilities.

item.title: CSS-Mamba: Competitive Sparse-Selection Mamba Network for Hyperspectral–LiDAR Joint Classification
item.dc:creator: Yongguang Zhai
Tingyuan Tan
Pingping Huang
Zhiguo Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data provide highly complementary information in terms of fine-grained spectral characterization and three-dimensional structural description, which is crucial for accurate land-cover classification in complex scenes. However, most existing multimodal fusion methods adopt isomorphic feature extraction and weighted aggregation strategies, which fail to explicitly model the intrinsic differences between spectral sparsity in HSI and geometric sparsity in LiDAR. Moreover, multi-scale fusion is often performed without explicit competitive selection mechanisms, leading to redundant feature accumulation and insufficient responses at discriminative scales. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a Competitive Sparse-Selection Mamba Network (CSS-Mamba) for hyperspectral–LiDAR joint classification. The proposed framework integrates modality-specific sparse modeling with competitive multi-scale feature selection in a unified architecture. Specifically, a Multi-Scale Spectral–Spatial Sparse Encoding (MS-SSSE) module and a Multi-Scale Geometric Structure Sparse Encoding (MS-GSSE) module are designed to separately model the spectral sparsity of HSI and the structural sparsity of LiDAR. Learnable soft-thresholding operators are embedded into the network to impose sparsity constraints at the architectural level, enabling modality-differentiated feature representations. Furthermore, a Competitive Sparse Selection (CSS) module is introduced, which combines visual selective state-space blocks with spatial competitive selection to establish explicit competition among multi-scale features, adaptively enhancing discriminative scales while suppressing redundant ones. Extensive experiments conducted on three public benchmark datasets, including Trento, Houston 2013, and MUUFL, demonstrate that the proposed CSS-Mamba consistently outperforms representative CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based multimodal fusion meth... Published: 2026-06-09T19:52:19+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yongguang Zhai; Tingyuan Tan; Pingping Huang; Zhiguo Wang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3701423

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data provide highly complementary information in terms of fine-grained spectral characterization and three-dimensional structural description, which is crucial for accurate land-cover classification in complex scenes. However, most existing multimodal fusion methods adopt isomorphic feature extraction and weighted aggregation strategies, which fail to explicitly model the intrinsic differences between spectral sparsity in HSI and geometric sparsity in LiDAR. Moreover, multi-scale fusion is often performed without explicit competitive selection mechanisms, leading to redundant feature accumulation and insufficient responses at discriminative scales. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a Competitive Sparse-Selection Mamba Network (CSS-Mamba) for hyperspectral–LiDAR joint classification. The proposed framework integrates modality-specific sparse modeling with competitive multi-scale feature selection in a unified architecture. Specifically, a Multi-Scale Spectral–Spatial Sparse Encoding (MS-SSSE) module and a Multi-Scale Geometric Structure Sparse Encoding (MS-GSSE) module are designed to separately model the spectral sparsity of HSI and the structural sparsity of LiDAR. Learnable soft-thresholding operators are embedded into the network to impose sparsity constraints at the architectural level, enabling modality-differentiated feature representations. Furthermore, a Competitive Sparse Selection (CSS) module is introduced, which combines visual selective state-space blocks with spatial competitive selection to establish explicit competition among multi-scale features, adaptively enhancing discriminative scales while suppressing redundant ones. Extensive experiments conducted on three public benchmark datasets, including Trento, Houston 2013, and MUUFL, demonstrate that the proposed CSS-Mamba consistently outperforms representative CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based multimodal fusion meth...

item.title: From Training-Free to Adaptive: Empirical Insights into MLLMs’ Understanding of Detection Information
item.dc:creator: Qirui Jiao
Daoyuan Chen
Yilun Huang
Yaliang Li
Ying Shen
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Computer Vision
item.description: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit impressive general-purpose capabilities, but struggle with fine-grained visual details. To mitigate this issue, a common remedy is to infuse textual information from specialized detectors (e.g., object coordinates, OCR results). However, this is typically done in a training-free manner. There is limited research on the effects of adaptive training, which has great potential for helping LLMs better comprehend the special input. This paper investigates a critical question: How does training shape an MLLM’s ability to leverage textual detection information? With representative models, we conduct a systematic study comparing three strategies: training-free infusion, full retraining, and lightweight fine-tuning. Our findings, evaluated across 10 benchmarks, reveal that a simple fine-tuning strategy is remarkably effective, outperforming its training-free counterpart by a significant margin (e.g., 6.71% average improvement). We uncover three key mechanisms behind this success: (1) Fine-tuning enhances robustness, enabling the MLLM to filter noise from imperfect detector outputs. (2) Unlike retraining from scratch, fine-tuning preserves the MLLM’s pre-trained multimodal feature synergy, avoiding performance degradation on general vision tasks. (3) Crucially, fine-tuning teaches the MLLM to understand the abstract semantics of detection information, allowing it to generalize to unseen detectors and novel information formats. Our work provides empirical evidence that adaptive fine-tuning is a vital step for effectively integrating external symbolic knowledge into MLLMs. We release our code to facilitate future research in this direction. Published: 2026-06-10T17:20:39+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.791 (must_read)
